questions.scenario: A team has one Pipeline job pointed at `main`. Feature branches do not get their own CI jobs, and pull requests are easy to miss. What does a Multibranch Pipeline add?

options.text: It scans the Git source, finds branches and pull requests with a Jenkinsfile, and creates a job for each one.
It moves all Jenkinsfiles into `$JENKINS_HOME` so branches no longer need one.
It runs every branch on the controller so agents are not needed.
It replaces Git webhooks with manual Build Now clicks.

questions.explanation: The article defines Multibranch Pipeline as the job type that scans a source repository and creates branch or PR sub-jobs for every branch or pull request that has a Jenkinsfile.

questions.scenario: A long-lived feature branch stops getting CI after the team added a Jenkinsfile on `main`. The branch still exists on GitHub, but the multibranch project does not list it. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The feature branch does not contain the Jenkinsfile at the configured path, so branch indexing skips it.
Jenkins never builds branches with slashes in their names.
Only `main` can use declarative pipelines.
The branch needs a production deploy before it can appear.

questions.explanation: The article calls this out directly: the Jenkinsfile must exist on every branch you want built. If a branch does not have the file yet, indexing has no pipeline entry point to create.

questions.scenario: Which considerations belong in a review of fork pull-request builds?

options.text: A malicious fork can modify the Jenkinsfile, so deploy credentials must not be exposed to arbitrary PR builds.
The safer default is to keep untrusted fork PRs away from privileged deploy stages.
The detailed trust model is security-sensitive enough that the credentials article covers it separately.
HTTPS cloning makes secrets safe to inject into every fork PR.

questions.explanation: The article says fork PRs are sandboxed by default and points to the credentials article for the trust model. The core principle is still visible here: PRs from forks are untrusted, so deploy credentials need stronger gates than ordinary branch builds.

questions.scenario: A repository has thousands of stale branches. Branch indexing now consumes noticeable controller memory and time. Which practice best fits the article?

options.text: Configure stale-branch retention to a short, intentional window and prune old branches.
Disable branch indexing forever.
Move branch indexing onto agents.
Put every branch's Jenkinsfile in a different directory.

questions.explanation: The article names branch indexing cost as an operational concern and recommends configuring stale-branch retention to a few weeks for large repositories.

questions.scenario: A multibranch project builds every branch, but all branches share almost identical Jenkinsfile logic. The team wants less duplication while keeping Jenkinsfile as the branch entry point. Which boundary is reasonable?

options.text: Keep a thin Jenkinsfile in each branch and move repeated pipeline logic into a shared library global step.
Delete every Jenkinsfile because shared libraries replace multibranch discovery.
Copy the same 200-line Jenkinsfile into every branch and accept the duplication.
Store the Jenkinsfile in the Jenkins UI instead of Git.

questions.explanation: The article frames the Jenkinsfile as the branch's pipeline contract. A thin wrapper can preserve that contract while delegating repeatable logic to a shared library, which the shared-libraries article expands in detail.
